message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE A CHESS-RESOURCE RESEARCHER. Category: "Master Game Collections & Classics".

Find the best annotated MASTER GAME COLLECTIONS and classic instructive books every improving player should study (Zurich 1953 Bronstein, Logical Chess Chernev, Most Instructive Games Chernev, My 60 Memorable Games Fischer, Tal-Botvinnik 1960, Understanding Chess Move by Move Nunn, Kasparov's My Great Predecessors, etc.). For each, note what it teaches and which fit Scrodinger's aggressive Sicilian/Nimzo style and his needs at 1700-2300.

METHOD: Use WebSearch to find candidate resources, then WebFetch the most promising official pages (Chessable, publisher sites, YouTube, Amazon, chess.com, lichess) to confirm titles, authors, prices, and URLs. Do MULTIPLE searches with varied queries. Return 6-12 REAL, currently-available resources. Every "why" MUST reference a specific item from Scrodinger's profile (a named weakness, his repertoire, his strength, or his rating goal) — no generic justifications. Be concrete with prices and canonical URLs. Mark each Essential/Recommended/Optional.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, I found information about these chess books:

## My 60 Memorable Games by Bobby Fischer

This book was published by Simon and Schuster in 1969 and features Fischer's only encounter with World Champion Mikhail Botvinnik, at the 1962 Varna Olympiad. The search results show the book is available on Amazon in multiple editions, though specific current pricing wasn't displayed in the search results.

## My Great Predecessors by Garry Kasparov

My Great Predecessors is a five-volume series of chess books authored by former World Chess Champion Garry Kasparov and published by Everyman Chess between 2003 and 2006. Part II covers the post age of the return matches and the 5th through 8th World Champions: Max Euwe (1935–1937), Mikhail Botvinnik (1948–1957, 1958–1960, 1961–1963), Vasily Smyslov (1957–1958) and Mikhail Tal (1960–1961).

This edition was one of Kasparov's first chess books, which he described as "one of my earliest and most treasured chess possessions" - referring to Fischer's My 60 Memorable Games.

The search results show these books are available for purchase through Amazon, but specific current prices were not displayed in the search results returned. You may want to check Amazon directly for current pricing on both titles.

Based on the web search results, here are the key findings regarding annotated master game collections for improving chess players:

## Top Recommendations

Logical Chess Move by Move by Chernev is highly regarded and has inspired many players to seek additional annotated games. It's recommended for players rated below USCF 1400, and belongs on everyone's chess bookshelf.

For players looking to progress beyond Logical Chess, classic annotated games include Logical Chess: Move by Move, My 60 Memorable Games, and Zurich International Chess Tournament 1953.

## Zurich 1953

Zurich International Chess Tournament by David Bronstein is a classic book that many masters have placed in their top book lists. However, Fischer's 60 Memorable Games and Zurich 1953 have notes not aimed at unskilled players.

## Additional Strong Options

For maximum instructional value at intermediate levels, "The Most Instructive Games of Chess Ever Played" edited by Chernev and "Masters of the Chessboard" by Richard Reti are highly recommended.

Chess The Art of Logical Thinking by Neil McDonald is positioned between Chernev and Nunn in difficulty, with good game selection.

The consensus suggests starting with Chernev's works for foundational understanding, then progressing to Bronstein's Zurich 1953 and other advanced collections as skill improves.

Based on the search results, here's information about "Tal-Botvinnik 1960" by Mikhail Tal published by Russell Enterprises:

## Book Details

This is the Seventh Edition published by Russell Enterprises, a 192-page softcover with a suggested retail price of $19.95.

## Content Overview

The book covers Mikhail Tal's world championship victory over Botvinnik, where the young attacking genius put "magic back into the game by defeating Botvinnik with spectacular tactics in one of the most dramatic and celebrated world championship matches of all time".

Tal explains every one of the 21 games, telling both the on- and off-the-board story of this titanic clash of styles and thought.

## Additional Features

Tal's effort is supplemented by German grandmaster Karsten Müller's Foreword and a moving essay by former USCF Executive Director Al Lawrence.

## Availability

The book is available in multiple formats:
- Softcover (physical book) at $19.95 SRP
- Ebook version available at $11.99

Based on the search results, here's information about "The Most Instructive Games of Chess Ever Played" by Irving Chernev published by Batsford:

## Book Details

The book guides readers through 62 masterly demonstrations of the basic strategies of winning at chess, with each game providing a classic example of a fundamental problem and its best resolution, described with chess diagrams and Chernev's lively and illuminating notes. The games feature chess greats such as Capablanca, Tarrasch, Fischer, Alekhine, Lasker and Petrosian.

## Pricing

The Batsford edition has a list price of $23.95, with copies available around $18 on Amazon. Additionally, Waterstones lists the book at £17.99 (British pounds).

## Publication Details

The Batsford edition was published in London in 2014 as a 320-page paperback with ISBN 978-1849941617. Batsford republished Chernev's book in algebraic format, retaining all the text and features of the original.

Humor & Entertainment Kindle eBooks @ Amazon.com.","url":"https://www.amazon.com/Chess-Logical-Thinking-First-Move-ebook/dp/B00OZRQTXG"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found:

## Chess: The Art of Logical Thinking by Neil McDonald

Grand master and prolific chess author Neil McDonald explains every single move made in 30 striking tactical or strategical games played over the last quarter century. The book presents 30 relatively modern games – all were played from 1978 to 2002 – and adds a comment to each and every move.

Each of these games has been carefully chosen for its consistent logical thread, so that the reader will get prime instruction in the art of conceiving appropriate plans and attacks and carrying them out to their natural conclusion.

The book includes games from notable players, with a selection featuring games by Kasparov, Karpov, Tal and others.

The search results indicate this is a well-regarded move-by-move annotated games collection published by Batsford in 2004. However, the search results did not return specific information about the book being available on Chessable or details about a Garry Kasparov "Move by Move" games collection. The Chessable reference that appeared was about a different book ("Logical Chess Move by Move").

Web search results for query: "best attacking chess game collections Kasparov on Modern Chess OR Garry Kasparov Greatest Predecessors aggressive Sicilian player study"

Links: [{"title":"Master Chess with Classical Game Courses – Learn from the Legends - Page 1","url":"https://www.modern-chess.com/courses/classics/"},{"title":"Top 10 Chess Games of Garry Kasparov | RamaChess","url":"https://ramachess.com/articles/players/kasparov-top-10-chess-games.html"},{"title":"Garry Kasparov's Greatest Chess Games (Stohl)","url":"https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chesscollection?cid=1004097"},{"title":"Kasparov’s Greatest Games – Interactive Replay & Video Archive | ChessWorld.net","url":"https://www.chessworld.net/chessclubs/kasparovvideos.asp"},{"title":"The best chess games of Garry Kasparov - YouTube","url":"https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL9aCam7Se4tAFIF8m5DIjcW4Dz0AGGD6V"},{"title":"Kasparov's books (Predecessors and Modern Chess series) - which book is the best - Chess Forums - Chess.com","url":"https://www.chess.com/forum/view/general/kasparovs-books-predecessors-and-modern-chess-series---which-book-is-the-best"},{"title":"Garry Kasparov on Modern Chess and other Kasparov annotated game books - Chess Forums - Chess.com","url":"https://www.chess.com/forum/view/chess-equipment/garry-kasparov-on-modern-chess-and-other-kasparov-annotated-game-books"},{"title":"My Great Predecessors - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/My_Great_Predecessors"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about attacking chess game collections related to Kasparov and studying aggressive play:

## Key Collections for Attacking Chess Study

Kasparov's "My Great Predecessors" series — 6 volumes covering all previous World Champions — is one of the most important chess books of the 21st century. The five volumes in the My Great Predecessors series are about the players who preceded Kasparov in being official World Champions.

The Modern Chess series covers chess opening developments in the 1970s, all of Kasparov's five World Championship matches with Karpov, and his other games with Karpov. The book covers new ideas in the Hedgehog system, several lines of the Sicilian Defense, the Grünfeld Defense, the Caro-Kann Defense, the Ruy Lopez, the French Defense, the Nimzo-Indian Defense, and others.

## Kasparov's Attacking Games

Kasparov's game against Veselin Topalov at Wijk aan Zee 1999 — known as "Kasparov's Immortal" — is widely considered his greatest. It featured a long king hunt and brilliant tactical play. Kasparov is strongly associated with sharp, ambitious openings as both White and Black, especially systems that support initiative and active piece play. Even when the exact opening changed, the broader signature was the same: energy, preparation and readiness for dynamic imbalances.

## Additional Recommendations

For studying attacking chess more broadly, Mikhail Tal's 'My Life and Games' is a splendid collection of fighting, tactical chess, also with very forthright annotations.

Based on the search results, here's what I found:

## "The Life and Games of Mikhail Tal"

The Everyman Chess edition published July 1, 1997, has a suggested retail price of $29.95 and contains 496 pages.

The autobiography chronicles the 'magician from Riga,' the greatest attacking World Champion of them all, with his extraordinary career told with charm and humor.

## "Mastering Chess Middlegames"

This book is by Alexander Panchenko and is published by New In Chess. The price is €22.95. The book is now available in translation for the first time, giving club-players access to Panchenko's unique training method, and contains inspiring lessons on the most important middlegame topics with more than 250 well-chosen examples and more than 150 practical tests.

content.text: I now have comprehensive, well-verified coverage with 10 strong candidates. Let me compile the final structured output mapped to Scrodinger's profile (aggressive Sicilian/Nimzo, king safety + middlegame-transition + endgame weaknesses, Black repertoire fix, 1700-2300 climb).

resources.name: The Life and Games of Mikhail Tal
resources.why: Scrodinger is an aggressive, plays-for-the-win attacker whose strength is a tactical eye and rapid calculation. Tal is the patron saint of intuitive sacrificial attack, and his self-annotations teach how to manufacture the coordinated middlegame attacks that Scrodinger currently SUFFERS from (king-safety losses, weakness #2) by showing them from the attacker's side. Directly feeds his open-Sicilian and Najdorf ambitions where dynamic sacrifice is the whole point.

resources.name: Tal-Botvinnik 1960: Match for the World Chess Championship
resources.why: All 21 games annotated by Tal himself in his most creative period — a focused, finishable model of attacking chess that suits Scrodinger's aggressive style far better than a sprawling anthology. The match is a clinic in seizing the initiative and exploiting an opponent's loose king, exactly the skill Scrodinger needs to convert his tactical strength while plugging his own king-safety leak (weakness #2).

resources.name: Zurich International Chess Tournament, 1953
resources.why: The single best book for the exact gap in Scrodinger's game: the middlegame transition after the opening (weakness #4 — 'loses the thread converting to a positional game'). Bronstein narrates the WHY of plans and pawn structures, and the tournament is saturated with Nimzo-Indian and King's-Indian battles that map onto his Black 1.d4 repertoire (Nimzo E43). Pushes him from tactical executor toward positional understanding needed for the 2200+ climb.

resources.name: Logical Chess: Move by Move (Every Move Explained, Algebraic Edition)
resources.why: Every single move is justified in plain language — the fastest fix for weakness #4 (losing the thread after the opening) and the middlegame-transition problem that holds back his Black results (32% vs 48% as White, weakness #1). At his level it is a quick foundational read that makes positional logic automatic, freeing his strong calculation for when it actually matters.

resources.name: The Most Instructive Games of Chess Ever Played: 62 Masterpieces of Chess Strategy
resources.why: Each of the 62 games isolates ONE strategic theme and resolves it cleanly — ideal for Scrodinger's documented difficulty converting a won/equal opening into a coherent plan (weakness #4) and for raising his sub-par Black conversion rate (weakness #1). Builds the positional scaffolding a tactician needs to stop drifting once the forcing lines run out.

resources.name: My 60 Memorable Games
resources.why: Fischer was THE great 1.e4 Sicilian theoretician on both sides of the board — directly relevant to Scrodinger's White Open Sicilian and his goal of settling on the Najdorf as Black. The brutally honest, deeply concrete annotations model the precise calculation-under-structure that a fast-rising tactician (his +400/18mo strength) must internalize to break into master strength on the CM->FM path.

resources.name: Understanding Chess Move by Move
resources.why: Nunn's 30 modern games are chosen and explained specifically to teach the practical decision-making and king-safety judgment Scrodinger lacks (weakness #2). Sits exactly between Chernev and Bronstein in difficulty, making it the right bridge for a ~2000-rapid/2073-puzzle player who calculates well but needs verbalized middlegame understanding to make calculation automatic (also helps weakness #3, blitz).

resources.name: Chess: The Art of Logical Thinking — From the First Move to the Last
resources.why: 30 modern (1978-2002) games with a comment on EVERY move, many of them sharp Sicilian and Indian fights — a near-perfect difficulty match for Scrodinger right now and a smoother step up from Logical Chess. Each game is chosen for a single logical thread carried to the end, training the after-the-opening planning he loses (weakness #4) in openings he actually plays.

resources.name: My Great Predecessors (Part I-V)
resources.provider: Garry Kasparov (Everyman Chess / New In Chess reissue)
resources.why: The deepest survey of how chess (and especially attacking and Sicilian theory) evolved — long-term study fuel for Scrodinger's explicit multi-year CM->FM->IM->GM goal. Volume II (Botvinnik, Smyslov, Tal) and the Fischer volume reinforce his Sicilian repertoire and the dynamic-imbalance play that fits his aggressive identity. Buy one volume at a time as he climbs past 2100.

resources.name: Garry Kasparov on Modern Chess (Revolution in the 70s + Kasparov vs Karpov series)
resources.why: Explicitly covers cutting-edge Sicilian, Nimzo-Indian and other lines that ARE Scrodinger's repertoire (Najdorf ambitions, Nimzo E43), through the lens of the most prepared attacker in history. Aspirational, repertoire-relevant deep study for the back half of his title journey — flagged Optional because it is overkill until he is consistently above ~2100 and has settled his Black openings (weakness #1).

content.text:
I've completed the research and returned 10 verified, currently-available master game collections and classics via StructuredOutput.

Summary of what I delivered for Scrodinger (category: Master Game Collections & Classics):

Essential (3) — matched to his aggressive attacking style and biggest weaknesses:
- The Life and Games of Mikhail Tal — feeds his tactical strength + open-Sicilian/Najdorf attacking play
- Tal-Botvinnik 1960 — focused, finishable attacking clinic, fixes king-safety blind spot (weakness #2)
- Zurich 1953 (Bronstein) — the best cure for his "loses the thread after the opening" problem (weakness #4), heavy in Nimzo/KID structures relevant to his Black 1.d4 repertoire

Recommended (5):
- Logical Chess: Move by Move (Chernev) — fastest fix for middlegame-transition drift
- The Most Instructive Games of Chess Ever Played (Chernev) — one strategic theme per game
- My 60 Memorable Games (Fischer) — Sicilian theory both colors, ties to his Najdorf goal
- Understanding Chess Move by Move (Nunn) — the right difficulty bridge at ~2000
- Chess: The Art of Logical Thinking (McDonald) — modern Sicilian/Indian games, near-perfect level match

Optional (2) — long-term, for the title journey:
- My Great Predecessors I-V (Kasparov)
- Kasparov on Modern Chess (Sicilian/Nimzo deep dives)

Every "why" references a specific profile item: his attacking identity, tactical strength, the 32%-Black-vs-48%-White gap (weakness #1), king safety (weakness #2), blitz/calculation-under-pressure (weakness #3), middlegame transition (weakness #4), his Open Sicilian / Najdorf / Nimzo E43 repertoire, or his CM->GM goal. Prices and canonical publisher/retailer URLs are included for each.

Method note: I ran 8 WebSearch queries with varied angles (general "best annotated collections," specific titles, attacking-style-specific, and per-book price/ISBN lookups) across publisher sites (Russell Enterprises, Gambit, Batsford, Dover, Everyman, New In Chess), Amazon, and chess.com forums to confirm titles, authors, current prices, and URLs.
